basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-ʿAlaq — “The Blood Clot” — bears the first five verses the Quran ever spoke. The scene is famous: the Prophet in retreat in the cave of Ḥirāʾ, Gabriel appearing, pressing him against his chest, commanding: “Recite!” But what must be heard here is that the very first word of the Book is an imperative, and that the second is in the Name of thy Lord. Every true reading begins this way: not from a project of one's own, but from a Call received, and from the pronouncing of a Name that is not one's own. The sūra, in nineteen verses, draws the full arc of the human condition: on one hand the creature drawn from a clot and taught by the Pen (vv. 1–5), on the other the rebellion (ṭughyān, طُغْيَان) born of the illusion of self-sufficiency (vv. 6–7), and at the end the return toward God — for whoever consents to it. Qushayrī comments on the final command, fasjud wa-qtarib (“prostrate and draw near”): “Draw near with the heart to the witnessing of Lordship, and stand with the soul on the carpet of servitude. It is also said: prostrate by the soul, and draw near by the secret of the heart (sirr, سِرّ).” Prostration is the work of the outside; nearness is the work of the within.

versets.trad: Recite in the Name of your Lord who created,
versets.commentaire: The very first word of the Quran is an imperative: iqraʾ (اقْرَأْ), “recite”, “read”, “proclaim”. And the first thing that follows is: “in the Name of your Lord”. Grammar already says the whole spiritual order to come: a man receives the order to read before knowing what is to be read; and when he begins, the first name pronounced is not his own, but his Lord's. The whole life of faith holds already in this posture: to respond before understanding, and to name God before naming oneself. The word Rabb (رَبّ) — Lord — is not the distant Master: He is the One who raises (rabbā), nourishes, makes grow. The Quran opens, by its very first words, within the safety of a divine upbringing.

mots.def: qaraʾa: to read aloud, to recite, to proclaim; imperative. Same root as Qurʾān.
preposition bi- + ism (name) in the construct genitive: “in the Name of”.
rabb + suffix pronoun: your Lord — the One who raises, nourishes, makes grow.
khalaqa: to create, to shape, to give existence; perfect.

versets.trad: created man from a blood clot.
versets.commentaire: ʿalaq (عَلَق) — which gives the sūra its name — names the blood clot, that which is suspended, hung. It is the third stage of gestation in the Quranic account: dust, drop, clot, lump of flesh, bone, flesh (cf. 22:5; 23:14). The sūra thus says in its first words what its stake will be: that man, born of a clot, should have been raised to receive the Book — this disproportion is meant to bring him to humility.

mots.def: ʿalaq: what hangs, what is suspended; the clot of blood, third stage of embryonic gestation in the Quran.

versets.trad: Recite! For your Lord is most noble,
versets.commentaire: The command is repeated — iqraʾ — as one insists with someone hesitating. The motive shifts: no longer “your Lord who created”, but “your Lord is al-akram (الأَكْرَم) — the most noble, the most generous”. Karam (كَرَم) named in pre-Islamic Arabia the highest of human virtues: nobility of lineage, magnanimity, generosity without limit. The Quran applies the word to God — a thing unheard of in pre-Islamic poetry. Through creation and revelation, He becomes the Master of generosity.

mots.def: akram: superlative of karīm; the most noble, the most generous. Karam designates in Arabic nobility of heart and boundless generosity.

versets.commentaire: al-qalam (القَلَم): the pen, the cut reed used to write. The verse names the very invention of writing as a divine gift. Commentators distinguish three pens: that of the Logos or Intellect, which God commanded to write all that would be until the Day of Resurrection; those of the angels who record human deeds; and those with which men write. Teaching “by the pen” is therefore at once cosmic and earthly — it is what makes the transmission of knowledge beyond a single life possible.

mots.def: ʿallama: intensive of ʿalima (to know); to teach, to convey knowledge.
qalam: the pen, the cut reed for writing; a symbol of writing as gift.

versets.trad: taught man what he knew not.
versets.commentaire: The verse closes the opening: God created man from a clot (v. 2); He teaches him what he did not know. By knowledge the tiny creature becomes noble. Adam, in the Quranic tradition, learned “the names of all things” (2:31), and that is why he was preferred to the angels. Man, then, was born for knowing — and knowing is gift, not conquest. According to some, these first five verses — the whole of the first revelation — are also a programme: God creates, God teaches, man receives.

mots.def: ʿalima: to know; apocopated imperfect after lam.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.commentaire: From here the sūra changes breath: verses 6 to 19 were revealed years later, in answer to Abū Jahl, hostile leader against the Prophet. kallā (كَلَّا): “nay!” — particle of rupture, which contradicts what one might think in view of the listed blessings. And the point: yaṭghā (يَطْغَىٰ), “he oversteps”, “he overflows” — the root ṭ-gh-y evokes water that leaves its bed, the animal that bolts, pride that no longer contains itself. Having received so much, man does not become grateful; he becomes rebellious.

mots.def: ṭaghā: to overflow, to overstep; to rebel against the order.

versets.trad: as soon as he sees himself self-sufficient.
versets.commentaire: istaghnā (اسْتَغْنَىٰ): to see oneself rich of self, to need nothing. The word says, in a single verb, the spiritual root of every rebellion. As long as the soul knows itself poor, it remains under the rain of the gift; as soon as it believes itself sufficient, it dries itself out. The Sufis call this acknowledged poverty iftiqār (افْتِقَار) — neediness consented before God — and they hold it for the very condition of light. The verse, in one word, overturns every possible pride: what man takes for his independence is, in truth, the moment when he deprives himself of the One who bears him.

mots.def: raʾā: to see; perfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: istaghnā: to be without need, to consider oneself self-sufficient; perfect. Root gh-n-y: wealth, sufficiency.

versets.trad: Truly to your Lord is the return.
versets.commentaire: al-rujʿā (الرُّجْعَىٰ): the return. A single sentence to recall the great Quranic law: “Truly, we are God's, and to Him we return” (2:156). Whatever man does with his illusory self-sufficiency, he does not stay where he thinks himself settled. All things go to Him.

mots.def: rujʿā: the return, the going back; verbal noun of rajaʿa.

versets.trad: Have you seen him who prohibits
versets.commentaire: The tone sharpens. a-raʾayta (أَرَأَيْتَ), “have you seen?”, calls attention to a particular case. Tradition unambiguously identifies him: Abū Jahl, who prevented the Prophet from praying at the Kaʿba.

mots.def: interrogative particle + raʾā in the perfect.
mots.gram: particle + perfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: a servant [of God] when he prays?
versets.commentaire: ʿabd (عَبْد): servant, worshipper. The word, indefinite, here names the Prophet at prayer — but by its generality, any believer one would prevent from praying. To prevent prayer, in the Quran, is among the gravest acts of denial.

mots.def: ʿabd: servant, worshipper; a human in the posture of adoration before God.

versets.trad: Have you seen if he is on guidance,
versets.commentaire: The verse turns the question back against the one who forbids: “Have you seen if, perchance, the one you prevent was on guidance?” al-hudā (الهُدَى): the guidance, the straight path. The very doubt should be enough to stop the gesture.

mots.gram: particle + perfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: hudā: right direction, guidance; verbal noun of hadā (to guide).

versets.commentaire: al-taqwā (التَّقْوَى): piety, awareness of God, reverential fear; from the verb waqā (to guard, to ward off). The word is central in the Quran — it names less an emotion than a vigilance of the heart toward what is owed to God. The verse extends the irony: “and if, on top of this, he enjoined piety?” Has the adversary not placed himself in an untenable position?

mots.def: taqwā: piety, awareness of God, respectful vigilance; from waqā, to guard.

versets.trad: Have you seen if he denied and turned away?
versets.commentaire: kadhdhaba (كَذَّبَ): to hold as a lie, to deny. tawallā (تَوَلَّى): to turn away, to turn one's back. The two verbs name the typical posture of the denier in the Quran: not merely not to believe, but actively to treat as lie and to flee. Verse 14 will settle the matter.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: intensive form; to give the lie to, to hold as liar.
tawallā: to turn away, to turn one's back.

versets.trad: Does he not know that God sees?
versets.commentaire: A question thrown like lightning. Allāhu yarā (اللَّهَ يَرَىٰ): “God sees”. The adversary acts as if no one saw. Yet nothing escapes the divine gaze — neither the public act of preventing prayer, nor the hidden intention that animates it. The verse suspends the sūra before the sentence of v. 15.

mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: كَلَّا لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ لَنَسْفَعًا بِالنَّاصِيَةِ
versets.tr: kallā la-in lam yantahi la-nasfaʿan bi-n-nāṣiya
versets.trad: Nay! If he ceases not, We shall surely seize him by the forelock,
versets.commentaire: al-nāṣiya (النَّاصِيَة): the forelock, the lock of hair on the brow. To seize someone by the forelock, in old Arabia, was to take him in total humiliation — a slave being dragged. Elsewhere the Quran: “Every creature, God holds it by its forelock” (11:56). The verse warns: if Abū Jahl does not cease, this is how he will be handled on the Day of Judgment.

mots.def: intahā: to cease, to stop; apocopated imperfect.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: safaʿa: to seize and drag forcefully; energetic 1st pers. plur.
nāṣiya: the lock of hair on the brow; to seize by the forelock, total humiliation.

versets.commentaire: The verse specifies: not any forelock — a “lying” and “iniquitous” one. As if the organ bore the guilt of its owner. A remarkable Quranic stylistic trait: the part for the whole, and the part as accused.

mots.def: kādhib: lying; active participle of kadhaba.
khāṭiʾ: erring, sinning; active participle of khaṭiʾa.

versets.trad: So let him call his cohorts!
versets.commentaire: nādī (نَادِي): the assembly, the club, the group of his peers. Tradition reports that Abū Jahl had boasted to the Prophet: “You know there is no one in Mecca with more men than I.” The verse replies with irony: let him call them, then, his “men”.

mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: nādī: assembly, club, group; the people one calls upon to witness or support.

versets.trad: We shall call the guards of Hell!
versets.commentaire: al-zabāniya (الزَّبَانِيَة): the guards of the Fire — the angels in charge of Hell. To the call of his human cohorts answers the call of the heavenly guards. The disproportion is plain: not the same forces are summoned. According to Ibn ʿAbbās, “by God, had he called his cohorts, God would have sent the guards of Hell against him.”

mots.def: daʿā: to call; apocopated imperfect 1st pers. plur.
zabāniya: the angels who guard Hell.

versets.trad: Nay! Obey him not! Prostrate and draw near [to God]!
versets.commentaire: The sūra closes on three imperatives — and Qushayrī, who comments only a few verses, does not let this one pass. Wa-qtarib, he says: “Draw near with the heart to the witnessing of Lordship, and stand with the soul on the carpet of servitude (ʿubūdiyya, عُبُودِيَّة).” Then, distinguishing the two orders, he adds: “Prostrate by the soul, and draw near by the secret of the heart (sirr, سِرّ).” Prostration engages the outside — the limbs, the ground, the forehead against the earth; nearness, however, is the work of the innermost within, where God alone sees. The exterior lowers itself so that the interior may rise. And the gesture has its answer: a ḥadīth says that “the closest a servant can be to his Lord is when he is prostrating.” This verse is among the fifteen after which a prostration is recommended — so that the sense of the text may find its place in the reader's body.

mots.def: aṭāʿa: to obey; here negative apocopated imperative + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: sajada: to prostrate, to place the brow on the ground; imperative.
iqtaraba: to draw near, to become close; imperative.
